title: PowerView — AD Enumeration Command Guide
body: PowerView is the fast manual layer between basic net.exe commands and full BloodHound graphing. Use it to enumerate users, groups, computers, shares, sessions, SPNs, and ACLs, then use the output to validate or challenge BloodHound paths.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# POWERVIEW COMMAND GUIDE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Authorized lab / assessment use only.
# Assumption: you are on a domain-joined Windows host with domain credentials.

# ── 0) IMPORT POWERVIEW ───────────────────────────
powershell -ep bypass
cd C:\Tools
Import-Module .\PowerView.ps1

# Quick check: confirm functions loaded.
Get-Command Get-Domain
Get-Command Get-DomainUser
Get-Command Get-DomainComputer

# ── 1) DOMAIN + DC INFO ───────────────────────────
Get-Domain
Get-NetDomain
Get-DomainController
Get-NetDomainController
Get-DomainPolicy
(Get-DomainPolicy)."System Access"

# Useful policy fields:
# - MinimumPasswordLength
# - PasswordComplexity
# - LockoutBadCount
# - ResetLockoutCount
# - LockoutDuration

# ── 2) USERS ──────────────────────────────────────
Get-DomainUser
Get-NetUser

# Clean user list.
Get-DomainUser | select samaccountname,cn,description

# User timing: dormant, stale, or recently used accounts.
Get-DomainUser | select samaccountname,pwdlastset,lastlogon,lastlogontimestamp

# User descriptions: common place for password leaks.
Get-DomainUser -Properties samaccountname,description |
  ? {$_.description} |
  select samaccountname,description

# Privileged / protected users.
Get-DomainUser -AdminCount |
  select samaccountname,memberof,admincount

# Full details for one user.
Get-DomainUser -Identity stephanie -Properties *

# Users with SPNs: Kerberoast candidates.
Get-DomainUser -SPN |
  select samaccountname,serviceprincipalname

# Users without Kerberos preauth: AS-REP roast candidates.
Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired |
  select samaccountname,useraccountcontrol

# ── 3) GROUPS + NESTED MEMBERSHIP ─────────────────
Get-DomainGroup | select cn
Get-NetGroup | select cn

# High-value groups.
Get-DomainGroupMember "Domain Admins"
Get-DomainGroupMember "Enterprise Admins"
Get-DomainGroupMember "Administrators"
Get-DomainGroupMember "Account Operators"
Get-DomainGroupMember "Server Operators"
Get-DomainGroupMember "Backup Operators"
Get-DomainGroupMember "Remote Desktop Users"

# Recursive membership catches nested groups.
Get-DomainGroupMember "Domain Admins" -Recurse
Get-DomainGroupMember "Management Department" -Recurse

# Group object details.
Get-DomainGroup "Sales Department" | select member
Get-DomainGroup "Development Department" | select member
Get-DomainGroup "Management Department" | select member

# Find groups a user belongs to.
Get-DomainUser -Identity stephanie -Properties memberof |
  select -ExpandProperty memberof

# ── 4) COMPUTERS + OPERATING SYSTEMS ──────────────
Get-DomainComputer
Get-NetComputer

# Clean host inventory.
Get-DomainComputer |
  select dnshostname,operatingsystem,operatingsystemversion

# Resolve hostnames if available.
Get-DomainComputer | select dnshostname | % { nslookup $_.dnshostname }

# Domain controllers.
Get-DomainController | select name,IPAddress,OSVersion

# Delegation checks.
Get-DomainComputer -Unconstrained |
  select dnshostname,useraccountcontrol

Get-DomainComputer -TrustedToAuth |
  select dnshostname,msds-allowedtodelegateto

# ── 5) LOCAL ADMIN ACCESS ─────────────────────────
# Find where current user has local admin.
Find-LocalAdminAccess

# Check specific machine if needed.
Find-LocalAdminAccess -ComputerName CLIENT74

# Why this matters:
# local admin on a machine + high-value session on same machine = possible path.

# ── 6) LOGGED-ON USERS + SESSIONS ─────────────────
# These may fail on modern Windows due to permissions.
Get-NetSession -ComputerName files04
Get-NetSession -ComputerName files04 -Verbose

Get-NetLoggedon -ComputerName client74
Get-NetLoggedon -ComputerName client74 -Verbose

# Hunt where a specific user may be logged in.
Find-DomainUserLocation -UserName jeffadmin

# Hunt privileged users' locations.
Invoke-UserHunter
Invoke-UserHunter -CheckAccess

# ── 7) SHARES ─────────────────────────────────────
Find-DomainShare
Find-DomainShare -CheckShareAccess

# Search interesting files in accessible shares.
Find-InterestingDomainShareFile

# Manual share checks.
ls \\dc1\SYSVOL
ls \\dc1\SYSVOL\corp.com\Policies
ls \\FILES04\docshare

# ── 8) SPNs + SERVICE ACCOUNTS ────────────────────
Get-DomainUser -SPN |
  select samaccountname,serviceprincipalname

# Native Windows SPN query.
setspn -L iis_service

# Resolve SPN host.
nslookup web04.corp.com

# ── 9) ACL ENUMERATION ────────────────────────────
# Important rights:
# GenericAll, GenericWrite, WriteOwner, WriteDacl,
# AllExtendedRights, ForceChangePassword, Self/AddMember.

# ACLs on one user or group.
Get-ObjectAcl -Identity stephanie -ResolveGUIDs
Get-ObjectAcl -Identity "Management Department" -ResolveGUIDs

# Clean dangerous ACL view.
Get-ObjectAcl -Identity "Management Department" -ResolveGUIDs |
  ? {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|WriteOwner|AllExtendedRights|ForceChangePassword|Self"} |
  select IdentityReferenceName,ActiveDirectoryRights,ObjectDN

# Broad hunt for interesting domain ACLs.
Find-InterestingDomainAcl -ResolveGUIDs

# Targeted ACLs for current user.
Find-InterestingDomainAcl -ResolveGUIDs |
  ? {$_.IdentityReferenceName -match "stephanie"} |
  select IdentityReferenceName,ActiveDirectoryRights,ObjectDN

# Convert SIDs when output is unreadable.
Convert-SidToName S-1-5-21-0000000000-0000000000-0000000000-1104

# ── 10) QUICK POWERVIEW TRIAGE ORDER ──────────────
# Run this order when you first land on a domain client.
Get-Domain
(Get-DomainPolicy)."System Access"
Get-DomainUser | select samaccountname,description
Get-DomainGroupMember "Domain Admins" -Recurse
Get-DomainComputer | select dnshostname,operatingsystem,operatingsystemversion
Get-DomainUser -SPN | select samaccountname,serviceprincipalname
Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired | select samaccountname
Find-LocalAdminAccess
Find-DomainShare -CheckShareAccess
Find-InterestingDomainAcl -ResolveGUIDs

# ── 11) DOCUMENTATION FORMAT ─────────────────────
# Finding:
# Evidence:
# Command:
# Output:
# Why it matters:
# Next action:
# Screenshot:
warn: PowerView is powerful but can be flagged in modern monitored environments. For OSCP-style labs, it is excellent for learning. Also remember: net.exe can miss nested groups; PowerView with recursive group enumeration is usually better for relationship mapping.

choices.label: Use these commands to validate BloodHound
